learningOutcomes: Recognise a quadratic equation in the form ax² + bx + c = 0
Identify the coefficients a, b, and c in a quadratic expression
Solve simple quadratic equations by factorisation
realLifeContexts: The path of a ball thrown through the air is a parabola
Calculating when a projectile hits the ground
Finding the dimensions of a rectangular area given its size
commonMisconceptions: A quadratic equation always has two different solutions
Factorisation only works when numbers are whole numbers

learningOutcomes: Solve quadratic equations using the quadratic formula
Determine the nature of roots using the discriminant
Solve quadratic equations by completing the square
Relate the roots to the graph of the quadratic function
realLifeContexts: Maximising the area of a garden with a fixed perimeter
Calculating the break-even points in a profit model
Designing the shape of a satellite dish or bridge arch
Optimising the trajectory of a launched object
commonMisconceptions: The discriminant being negative means no answer exists in any number system
Completing the square only works for quadratics with even b coefficients

learningOutcomes: Form a quadratic equation given its roots using Vieta's formulas
Solve problems involving quadratic inequalities
Analyse quadratic functions and their transformations
Apply quadratic models to optimisation problems
realLifeContexts: Revenue maximisation where revenue = price × quantity
Structural load calculations in engineering
Signal processing using quadratic approximations
Modelling stopping distance as a quadratic function of speed
commonMisconceptions: Quadratic inequalities are solved the same way as quadratic equations
Transformations of quadratics change the degree of the function

learningOutcomes: Solve one-step and two-step linear equations
Represent a linear relationship using a table, equation, and graph
Identify the gradient and y-intercept in y = mx + c
realLifeContexts: Calculating the total cost of items at a fixed price per unit
Converting between temperature scales (Celsius and Fahrenheit)
Working out how long a journey takes at constant speed
commonMisconceptions: The gradient of a line is always a positive number
You can add a number to one side of an equation without doing the same to the other

learningOutcomes: Solve simultaneous linear equations graphically and algebraically
Solve linear inequalities and represent solutions on a number line
Write and solve linear equations from word problems
Interpret gradient as a rate of change in context
realLifeContexts: Finding the break-even point where revenue equals cost
Comparing mobile phone plans using simultaneous equations
Speed cameras using linear graphs of distance vs time
Budget planning with income and expenditure constraints
commonMisconceptions: Simultaneous equations always have exactly one solution
When multiplying an inequality by a negative number, the direction stays the same

learningOutcomes: Solve systems of three linear equations using matrices or elimination
Apply linear programming to optimise an objective function
Model and solve real-world problems using systems of inequalities
Interpret solutions in context and assess their validity
realLifeContexts: Optimising a production schedule to maximise profit given resource constraints
Logistics routing problems using linear models
Nutritional planning meeting minimum daily requirements
Financial portfolio allocation under budget constraints
commonMisconceptions: Linear programming always yields integer solutions
Any point inside the feasible region gives the optimal solution

learningOutcomes: Classify triangles by sides (equilateral, isosceles, scalene) and angles (acute, right, obtuse)
State that the sum of angles in a triangle is 180°
Apply the properties of triangles to find missing angles
realLifeContexts: Triangular shapes in roof trusses and bridges for structural strength
The triangular shape of a set square used in technical drawing
Navigation using triangulation to find a position
commonMisconceptions: An obtuse triangle can also be a right-angled triangle
The exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of all interior angles

learningOutcomes: Apply Pythagoras' theorem to find missing sides in right-angled triangles
Use the conditions for congruence (SSS, SAS, ASA, RHS)
Identify and apply properties of similar triangles
Calculate the area of triangles using different formulas
realLifeContexts: Calculating the length of a ladder needed to reach a wall
Architects using similar triangles for scale drawings
Surveying land using triangulation and Pythagoras
Navigation systems computing shortest distance
commonMisconceptions: Pythagoras' theorem applies to all triangles, not just right-angled ones
Two triangles with the same area are always congruent

learningOutcomes: Apply the sine rule and cosine rule to non-right-angled triangles
Calculate the area of a triangle using (1/2)ab sin C
Prove geometric theorems involving triangles
Solve multi-step problems combining multiple triangle theorems
realLifeContexts: GPS satellite triangulation using the cosine rule
Calculating distances across rivers or valleys using the sine rule
Aircraft navigation using bearings and triangle calculations
3D computer graphics rendering using triangle mesh geometry
commonMisconceptions: The sine rule can always be used instead of the cosine rule
There is always only one possible triangle given two sides and a non-included angle

learningOutcomes: Define a polynomial and identify its degree, terms, and coefficients
Add and subtract polynomials by collecting like terms
Multiply a polynomial by a monomial
realLifeContexts: Calculating the perimeter of a field with algebraic side lengths
Expressing the area of a shape using polynomial expressions
Modelling population growth with a polynomial formula
commonMisconceptions: You can add terms with different variables or powers
The degree of a polynomial is the number of terms it has

learningOutcomes: Expand and simplify products of two binomials
Factorise polynomials including difference of squares and perfect square trinomials
Divide a polynomial by a linear expression
Apply the remainder theorem and factor theorem
realLifeContexts: Factorising to find the dimensions of a box given its volume as a polynomial
Using the factor theorem in cryptography and coding theory
Polynomial curve fitting in data analysis and forecasting
Signal processing using polynomial approximations
commonMisconceptions: Every polynomial can be factorised over the integers
The remainder theorem and factor theorem are the same result

learningOutcomes: Solve polynomial equations of degree three and four
Sketch polynomial functions and identify key features
Apply polynomial identities to simplify complex expressions
Use the rational root theorem to find exact roots
realLifeContexts: Modelling the shape of a roller coaster track using cubic polynomials
Engineering: stress and strain curves modelled by polynomials
Computer graphics using polynomial Bezier curves
Economic models using higher-degree polynomial cost functions
commonMisconceptions: A polynomial of degree n always has n distinct real roots
Turning points and roots of a polynomial always alternate

learningOutcomes: Collect and organise data into frequency tables and charts
Calculate mean, median, mode, and range for a data set
Express probability as a fraction, decimal, and percentage
realLifeContexts: Calculating the average score of a class on a test
Weather forecasts expressed as probability of rain
Sports statistics: batting averages and scoring rates
commonMisconceptions: The mean is always the best measure of average
A probability of 0.5 means the event will happen exactly half the time

learningOutcomes: Construct and interpret histograms, box plots, and scatter graphs
Calculate probabilities of combined events using AND/OR rules
Distinguish between mutually exclusive and independent events
Interpret correlation from scatter graphs and calculate lines of best fit
realLifeContexts: Medical research using statistics to test drug effectiveness
Insurance companies calculating risk using probability
Polling and election forecasting
Quality control in manufacturing using statistical sampling
commonMisconceptions: Correlation between two variables proves that one causes the other
If two events are not mutually exclusive they must be independent

learningOutcomes: Apply the normal distribution to model real-world data
Use conditional probability and Bayes' theorem
Conduct hypothesis tests and interpret p-values
Evaluate the validity and reliability of statistical conclusions
realLifeContexts: Clinical trials using hypothesis testing to approve new medications
Spam email filters using Bayesian probability
Quality control using control charts and standard deviation
Financial risk modelling using normal distribution
commonMisconceptions: A small p-value proves the alternative hypothesis is true
The normal distribution applies to all naturally occurring data

learningOutcomes: Plot and read coordinates in all four quadrants
Calculate the distance between two points
Find the midpoint of a line segment
realLifeContexts: Map reading using grid references
GPS coordinates locating places on Earth
Plotting data points in a spreadsheet chart
commonMisconceptions: The distance formula only works for horizontal or vertical lines
The midpoint is always the average of just the x-coordinates

learningOutcomes: Find the equation of a line given two points or a point and gradient
Determine whether lines are parallel or perpendicular
Calculate the area of shapes given coordinates of vertices
Solve geometric problems using coordinate methods
realLifeContexts: Urban planners using coordinates to calculate land areas
Robotics programming motion paths using coordinate equations
Computer graphics drawing lines and shapes using coordinates
Air traffic control using coordinate-based positioning
commonMisconceptions: Two lines with different equations always intersect at exactly one point
Perpendicular lines always have gradients that are equal and opposite

learningOutcomes: Derive and apply the equation of a circle
Find the intersection of lines and circles algebraically
Apply transformations using coordinate methods
Prove geometric results using coordinate geometry
realLifeContexts: Radar systems detecting objects within a circular range
Designing circular roundabouts and curved road sections
Satellite orbit paths modelled as circles or ellipses
Computer game collision detection using circle equations
commonMisconceptions: A tangent to a circle intersects it at two distinct points
All circles centred at the origin have the equation x² + y² = 1

learningOutcomes: Define sine, cosine, and tangent ratios in a right-angled triangle
Use trigonometric ratios to find missing sides and angles
Recall and apply the mnemonic SOH-CAH-TOA
realLifeContexts: Calculating the height of a building from its shadow and angle of elevation
Finding the angle of a ramp for wheelchair accessibility
Navigation: calculating the bearing between two points
commonMisconceptions: Trigonometric ratios only apply to 30-60-90 and 45-45-90 triangles
sin(A + B) = sin A + sin B

learningOutcomes: Apply trigonometry to angles greater than 90° using the unit circle
Sketch and interpret graphs of sin, cos, and tan functions
Use trigonometric identities to simplify expressions
Solve trigonometric equations within a given range
realLifeContexts: Modelling tidal patterns and seasonal temperature changes
Sound waves and musical notes as sinusoidal functions
AC electricity described by a sine wave
Analysing mechanical vibrations in engineering
commonMisconceptions: Radians and degrees are interchangeable without conversion
The period of any sinusoidal function is always 2π

learningOutcomes: Prove trigonometric identities using algebraic manipulation
Apply compound angle and double angle formulas
Solve complex trigonometric equations analytically
Model real-world periodic phenomena using trigonometric functions
realLifeContexts: Fourier analysis decomposing complex signals into sine waves
Phase relationships in electrical circuits (impedance)
Modelling ocean wave interference using compound angles
Satellite dish alignment using inverse trigonometric functions
commonMisconceptions: Proving an identity means solving it like an equation
Inverse trig functions always return a unique angle for any input

learningOutcomes: Calculate the area and perimeter of rectangles, triangles, and circles
Calculate the volume and surface area of cuboids
Convert between units of area and volume
realLifeContexts: Measuring the floor area of a room before buying carpet
Calculating the paint needed for a wall
Finding the capacity of a fish tank or storage box
commonMisconceptions: Area and perimeter increase together when a shape gets larger
Volume is measured in square units like area

learningOutcomes: Calculate the area and arc length of sectors
Find the volume and surface area of cylinders, cones, and spheres
Solve problems involving composite shapes
Apply Pythagoras' theorem in 3D mensuration problems
realLifeContexts: Calculating how much material is needed to make a tin can
Designing packaging to minimise waste while maximising volume
Calculating the volume of a water tower or storage tank
Estimating the material needed for a conical party hat
commonMisconceptions: The surface area and volume of a sphere use the same radius formula
Doubling all dimensions of a shape doubles its volume

learningOutcomes: Calculate the volume and surface area of pyramids and frustums
Apply integration to find areas bounded by curves
Solve optimisation problems involving area and volume
Model real-world objects using combined 3D shapes
realLifeContexts: Designing a dam spillway using frustum volume calculations
Calculating the volume of liquid in a tilted cylindrical tank
Minimising the material cost of a tin can with fixed volume
Modelling terrain volume for construction excavation estimates
commonMisconceptions: Scaling all linear dimensions by a factor k scales the volume by k²
Integration for area always gives a positive result
